title: Re-caning a pressed cane seat
subtitle: Remove the old sheet, clean the groove, press new cane in with spline and mallet
excerpt: How to replace a broken or sagging pressed cane seat panel: removing the old cane and spline, cleaning the groove, soaking the new sheet, pressing it into the groove, and securing with a new spline and adhesive.
sourceNotes: David James The Complete Guide to Upholstery. Traditional caning guides (public domain). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

recipeTools.notes: For trimming the cane sheet flush to the spline after the adhesive has set.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-woven mesh of rattan peel sold in sheets or by the metre. Fitted into a routed groove in the chair frame and held by a spline pressed over it. Faster to fit than hand-woven strand cane. The majority of modern caned-seat chairs use pressed cane.
A strip of rattan or fibre that is pressed into the groove on top of the cane sheet, locking it in place. Cut to length and glued with PVA or contact adhesive.

content.text: Pressed cane seats are replaced as a sheet, not rewoven strand by strand. The
sheet is available in several mesh sizes; measure the holes in the existing frame groove and buy the matching size. The matching
width is selected to fit the groove exactly.
Step 1, remove the old cane and spline

Use the utility knife to cut around the perimeter of the cane sheet just inside the groove. Prise out the old spline with a flat screwdriver or utility knife tip: it is usually held with old PVA and will lever out in sections. Remove all the old cane from the groove. Clean out the groove with a stiff brush and utility knife, removing all old adhesive and debris. The groove must be clean and dry for the new cane to seat properly.
Step 2, cut and soak the new cane sheet

Cut the cane sheet 50 mm larger than the seat opening in each direction to provide enough material to hold during pressing. Soak the sheet in warm water for 15 to 20 minutes until it is pliable and slightly soft. Also soak the new spline for 5 minutes. Wet cane is flexible and will seat cleanly into the groove; dry cane will crack when pressed.
Step 3, press the cane into the groove

Lay the wet cane sheet over the seat opening, aligning the weave pattern parallel to the frame. Begin pressing the cane into the groove at the mid-point of one side, using a wedge tool (a blunt-edged dowel or the handle of a craft knife) and the rubber mallet. Work to each corner, keeping the cane taut and the weave straight. Press the corners in last, folding the excess into a neat pleat. Maintain even tension: a loose centre will sag once the cane dries.
Step 4, glue and press the spline

Apply a bead of PVA wood glue into the groove on top of the pressed cane. Press the wet spline into the groove with the rubber mallet, tapping firmly along its length. The spline should be flush with or fractionally below the top face of the frame. Wipe off excess glue immediately.

Allow the glue to set fully (at least 4 hours, overnight is better). Trim the cane sheet flush to the outside edge of the spline with the craft knife or utility knife held at a low angle. As the cane dries over the following day, it will tighten and firm up.

attrs.symptom: Cane sheet sags in the centre after drying
attrs.cause: The cane was not held taut during pressing, or was pressed dry rather than soaked.
attrs.fix: Dampen the cane lightly with a sponge and allow it to re-dry under slight tension. If the sag is severe, remove and restart with a fresh sheet soaked for the full 15 minutes.

attrs.symptom: Spline keeps springing out before the glue sets
attrs.cause: Spline width is slightly too large for the groove, or the cane beneath is too thick.
attrs.fix: Use masking tape across the spline at 50 mm intervals to hold it down while the glue cures. If the spline is genuinely oversized, sand the back face lightly to reduce the thickness.
